title: Reasons Not to Bathe
description: Things got sticky this week, Stephen told her, when the man he called the Russian went into the bathroom for private ablutions and the glass eye he took out to wash slipped through his fingers and rolled into the toilet stall. The first thing anyone heard was the unlockable bathroom door swinging open with unusual force. Then the surge of limbs pumping and a sense of their pluming wake as the panicked one-eyed man charged fast to the nurses’ station, holding the eye aloft between two fingers
hook: A man's glass eye slips into a toilet. This opening promises the kind of strange, sensory specificity that makes mundane horror unforgettable.

title: Sue Tompkins’s Concrete Poetry Frees Words from Syntax
description: At King’s Leap, documents and audio works perform an “unspectacular yet remarkable” play with language
hook: Sue Tompkins breaks language free from grammar itself—words become objects, syntax becomes optional. Poetry that refuses to behave.

title: Low Culture Essay: Ledio Cakaj on Greek Laïko Music
description:
Ledio Cakaj relates how the “romantic longing” of Laïko sustained him as an undocumented Albanian teenager, living and working in Patras and Athens in the mid-90s

Dimitris Mitropanos' 1978 album, Parapono

Laïko (meaning ‘of the people’) is the popular music that has nursed Greek heartbreak since the middle of the 20th cent
hook: How Greek laïko music sustained an undocumented teenager in exile: low culture, high emotional stakes, diaspora longing made audible.

title: Shy Creatures: on Brief Encounter
description: And so, in the dream-logic of this particular shade, the express train in Brief Encounter is called the express not only because it doesn’t stop, but because, in its not-stopping, it expresses the shy heart of Laura Jesson.
hook: A train that doesn't stop becomes the perfect metaphor for a woman's trapped desire. Brief Encounter reimagined through mood and restraint.

title: You Have Nothing to Lose But Your Supply Chains!
description:
“Logistics,” warns Susan Zieger, “reveals how the illicit economy is the legitimate economy: the nature of free trade prevents the two from ever being separated.”

The post You Have Nothing to Lose But Your Supply Chains! appeared first on Public Books.
hook: The supply chain is where criminal and legitimate economies blur into one seamless operation. Logistics as the dark machinery of late capitalism.
